candidates.stmt:
I work in Ancoats every day at Generator X in Royal Mills. I have lived across east and south Manchester for over twenty years. I am not a career politician — I am a PhD holder, a mediator, and someone who has spent their career asking hard questions and getting answers. I am standing because this ward deserves a councillor who is already here, not one who turns up at election time.

Who I am:

I came to Manchester as a student and never left. Over two decades I have researched heart disease at the University of Manchester, trained as a legal professional, and worked in conflict resolution and community health. I know how systems work and how to challenge them when they stop working for people.

Why I love this ward:

Every morning I walk into Ancoats and see what a neighbourhood looks like when people build something with genuine care. The gym owner who transformed a community space in under a year. The cafe that knows every regular by name. The boutique stocking local designers nobody else will back. These businesses are not a backdrop to this ward — they are its identity. They are being squeezed by rates, costs, and a council that rarely asks what impact its decisions have on people already here. Supporting independent businesses will not be an afterthought for me. It will be a priority.

What I will focus on:

Residents do not need grand promises. They need someone who shows up and fights for what is practical and deliverable.
Homes in Beswick are being demolished right now. Families who have lived on Grey Mare Lane for decades are being moved out. The council promises they can return. A promise is not a guarantee. I will push for a written, binding commitment — not an aspiration.
More than six in ten residents in this ward have moved away in the last decade. Regeneration that prices out the people already here is not success. I will hold every planning application to a simple test: does this work for the people already living in this ward?

Why now:

The Green

**About The Candidate:**

Alex-Salik Imran is a student Nurse and union representative for the Royal College of Nursing.

As the Green Party candidate for Ardwick this year, he is committed to standing up and fighting for local residents in Ardwick.

**Candidate Statement:**

Ardwick has given me so much, a community, friends, and opportunities. Now I want to give something back.

I’ve been inspired by the work many local residents groups, faith organisations and charities do in the local area. These groups help to hold the area together and give people real hope.

I also understand that tackling the things that make daily life harder than it should be are a priority for our communities. Commuter traffic cutting through our streets, cars blocking residential estates and the persistent fly-tipping. As a student nurse, I see first-hand what broken glass, overflowing bins, and toxic air actually do to people’s bodies and wellbeing. These aren’t just eyesores. They’re health hazards. And Ardwick deserves better. A cleaner, greener healthier ward is what I’ll be fighting for locally.

Vote Green in Ardwick this May!

(This statement can be found at https://manchester.greenparty.org.uk/our-2026-local-election-candidates/#Ardwick).

Joe is proud to have been selected as the Liberal Democrat candidate for the Piccadilly Ward. He joined the party in 2017 after Jeremy Corbyn supported Theresa May in whipping the vote to enact Article 50 without a clearly defined strategy in place, a process which has been disastrous.

"For years I've had friends and family tell me that I should enter politics and that `I'd vote for you`, but I'd always dismissed that encouragement because I don't want to be in the public eye and I'd assumed that the people in charge nationally and locally ultimately had more experience and sense even if their public image suggested otherwise. Now though it's clear that, despite some notable exceptions, that just isn't the case.

"My job as an IT systems administrator requires me to juggle resolving problems that have come up, and making structural changes to systems where there is an underlying root cause to repeat issues, all whilst keeping in mind that `everything is connected` and that a change in one place might trigger something problematic somewhere else. I also have to translate requests for things people say that they want in to something that's actually deliverable, often by trying to rephrase the request in to finding out what it is that they're trying to achieve and then using my expertise and the expertise of other people who know better than me to design, test and implement a system that works. It's exactly the sort of approach that I think is needed in politics.

"I want to bring fresh thinking to a council dominated by a Labour party quick to close ranks in the face of any criticism and dismissive of any suggestion that this city could be run better. I want to make sure that the voices of the people who live in the city centre and who run small businesses here are heard and taken seriously, instead of only listening to property developers at industry junkets over drinks in Cannes. I want to see the city deliver 20% affordable housing in every development, and not ce

Originally from Poland, I have lived in Wythenshawe for two decades, lately in the area of Baguley. Over the years I often volunteered with local groups, reported numerous issues to the council and responded to local consultations, like the active travel scheme or Greater Manchester Spatial Framework Plans to name a few. I was one of the founding members and a secretary of Wythenshawe Waste Warriors regularly organising litter picking events throughout Wythenshawe.

I liaised with environmental charities like The City Of Trees, Groundwork or Lancashire Wildlife Trust as well as local businesses wanting to do voluntary work in the area. Visitors to Wythenshawe Hall see me helping with the monthly open days as I want the residents of Wythenshawe to have better access to cultural and heritage events.

Through my work I want the local community to feel happy, empowered and proud of where we live.

I’m a final-year student at the University of Manchester, studying environmental politics. I also have several years’ experience working with children with special educational needs. I’m originally from Leeds, but I’ve found a new home here on the other side of the Pennines.

I joined the Green Party in 2024 because I believe that social and environmental injustice have the same root causes, and we can’t fight them separately. I’ve had the most incredible time campaigning with the local party, including in Gorton and Denton, and I’m so honoured to be standing for Brooklands this May.

I’m standing as the Green Party candidate for Burnage because this is my home. I’ve lived here since I was two, I know these streets, I know the community, and I’ve seen the changes over time. Burnage sits on some of Manchester’s busiest routes, so traffic, congestion and air pollution affect us every day. Safety matters too, whether that’s road safety or knife crime, and too often these issues are raised but not properly addressed.

Burnage is a diverse ward and that should be a strength, but it needs more investment in community cohesion so people feel connected and supported. We need to properly support our small businesses, giving them the space and backing to grow. For too long, Labour has taken areas like Burnage for granted, with the same issues coming up again and again without real change. Burnage has a Green MP, now it’s time for that change to reach the council as well.

I work in communications and serve as a Campaigns Coordinator at an executive level within the Green Party, and co-founded Greens for Palestine.

Dear residents of Burnage,
My name is Muhammad Nadeem Tahir, and I am proud to stand as your Worker Party of Britain candidate for Burnage Ward Local Elections 2026.
I am a 31-year-old young candidate, and by profession I am a journalist and book writer. This is my first election, and I joined politics because I truly want to serve my community better and give residents a stronger voice.
Through my work as a journalist and writer, I have learned to listen to people, understand real-life problems, and speak up for fairness and justice. I believe our community deserves honest leadership that works for ordinary people.
Like many of you, I care deeply about the future of our neighbourhood, our families, and our young people. I believe local politics should serve the people and bring real improvements to our daily lives.
If elected, my priorities will be:
Improving local services and ensuring council resources are used fairly
Tackling anti-social behaviour and improving community safety
Supporting cleaner streets and better waste management
Standing up for affordable housing and protecting tenants
Supporting local businesses and community development
Being accessible, transparent, and accountable to residents
As a young candidate in my first election, I bring energy, fresh ideas, and dedication to serve our community. I promise to listen carefully to your concerns and work hard to make Burnage a safer, cleaner, and stronger place for everyone.
Vote Muhammad Nadeem Tahir
Worker Party of Britain  Burnage Ward 2026 🗳️

I joined Reform in 2022 because of the Con/Lab Uniparty’s disastrous handling of the pandemic. Working with small businesses in my job, I saw first-hand the effects this had on working families in Bolton and our entire Country, which are still being felt today.

We’ve seen tax hikes, open borders, a failing NHS, housing crisis, rising crime and the overwhelming feeling of our social fabric being ripped apart. All of this and more is why I joined Reform. First leafleting for a by-election - then standing as a candidate for local election campaigning for health centres in Bolton and fighting to get us out of the wasteful Greater Manchester Authority and back into Lancashire - to now humbly standing for Parliament.

Our party is now going from strength to strength under the leadership of Nigel Farage. We are witnessing the beginning of a real political movement that is not afraid to stand up for the silent majority.

I’m a working-class lad from the North. I’m not doing this for a career. I am doing this to put more money in your pocket, to deliver better NHS services and to ensure that our young people in Bolton will have the opportunity to own a home. Most of all, I am doing this because Bolton & our entire Country deserves better than the current toxic lot. If you want change, vote for it on July 4th. Vote Reform UK!

Manchester has so much to brag about, and when I graduate this year, there’s no place I’d rather be. But I’m also worried about finding a job that pays enough, or somewhere cheap and safe to live, or about surging energy costs. Yet it’s always those that work the hardest that suffer the most from them.

The Greens want to build a city that everyone can afford, where we can rely on help when we need it most, and a council rooted in community. I’ve seen that local government can change lives, and I want to help make sure that Charlestown residents’ lives are changed too.

I have been a member of the Green Party for well over forty years and have lived in Manchester for nearly all of that time. Over the years I have represented the Party in elections at local, Parliamentary and European level, and recently spent three years as Elections Coordinator on the Green Parties national executive.

Now retired, I formerly worked in Computing in the city centre.

My priorities include affordable housing, support for small businesses and improvement to the local environment including protecting green spaces and cleaner air. Our recent win in Gorton and Denton proves the Green Party is now a serious force in British politics. Cheetham is an area with a fascinating history and great potential, but it has been neglected – if elected I will fight to change that.

Councillors are part of the glue that hold communities together and represent them when they’re not heard. They help to platform voices, protect local spaces and prioritise the issues most important to local residents to get council money spent in the right places, be it potholes, school places or cycleways.

In everything I’ve done, community and collective action have been the driving force which has made change happen, made a difference to people’s lives and embodied the mantra of the Greens, hope not hate. The Green Party is about uniting communities, bringing people together and not pushing them apart – and where better to start then at the local level.

Having lived in Manchester for nearly 10 years, I understand the challenges our communities face, including insecure housing, rising living costs, and the impacts of over 25 years of austerity. As an environmental political researcher, I know how to turn community needs into local empowerment.

My work in local politics has deepened my commitment to supporting groups that advocate for better housing, access to green spaces, and greater inclusivity. I will always listen to Chorlton residents and prioritise their concerns. Chorlton is a vibrant community, as highlighted by efforts such as Save Rye Bank Fields and charities like Reach Out to the Community. As a councillor, I would extend this community ethos and promote a collaborative governance model where everyone has a say in decisions that affect our area.

Manchester’s changed, hasn’t it?

The only thing that doesn’t is that we have a council that still doesn’t get to grips with the basics. That’s why I’m standing for the Green Party: it’s time for everyday people like us to shake things up!

I’m sick of the “that’ll do” attitude. From fly-tipping to potholes, increasing energy costs, missed bin collections and uneven pavements – Clayton and Openshaw deserve better.

I work for charities that try to make things a little better. Vote for me, make hope normal, and I’ll make sure the council gets things right!
Former Petty Officer Weapons Engineer in the Royal Navy. Now working in cost intelligence.

Having lived in Crumpsall my whole life, I would do everything in my power to support and protect the local community. As someone who is working class, I understand the cost of living crisis and how its affecting those on lower wages. I am deeply passionate about improving the lives of those who are often overlooked or demonised by politicians.

One of the things I love most about Crumpsall is the diversity – with the rise of the far-right enabled by our Labour government, we need to protect every part of our community and stick together.

Here's my top three priorities

- Standing up for residents - I will campaign for rent controls, along with accountability for landlords and developers. Deansgate is a cultural centre, but it is home too.

- Fight for community spaces - I know how important clean air and green space is. I will work with community groups of local green spaces - working with the council to control litter, create play areas and more trees!

- Support culture - I will get stuck in with community groups and work with our cultural spaces to do more to support residents.

I have lived on Liverpool Road since 2005 and love the opportunities it presents to live in a modern, dynamic city while developing a connection with the community around me.

I was first elected as a City Centre Councillor in 2012 and re-elected in 2016. I was elected to the new Deansgate Ward in 2018. In my six years as a Councillor, I’ve concentrated on improving the ways the various city centre stakeholders operate, so that ours is a liveable city while losing none of its liveliness and energy. I’ve represented residents at Planning and Licensing hearings, not to stall development, but ensure that we can all work together.

I believe in the power of people and community organising. I have worked for the past 10 years in radical economic education, where I’m Executive Director of Rethinking Economics International. I also co-founded Greater Manchester Tenants Union, dedicating a great deal of my adult life to community organising in the city I love.

If I were to become councillor, I would work tirelessly to stop wasteful outsourcing, defend tenants rights and campaign to end the cost of living crisis.

I’ve called Manchester home for over a decade and care deeply about the future of our communities. I’ve worked in marketing and communications for more than 15 years, and I want to use those skills to **amplify the voices and concerns of West Didsbury residents**.

I’m standing as the Green Party candidate for Didsbury West, focusing on **protecting our green spaces, tackling traffic issues, and supporting residents through the cost of living crisis**, while making sure local people have a real say in decisions that shape our city.

I’m a public servant based in Greater Manchester. I have worked in local government across Greater Manchester, including Fallowfield, on neighbourhood services, homelessness commissioning, and climate and energy.

I believe climate action must reduce bills and improve lives, through warm homes, cleaner air, better transport and stronger communities locally.

To the Electors of Fallowfield,
Friends and Neighbours,

I write to you with a sincere respect for the trust you place in those who seek to represent you. In Fallowfield, we see both the energy of a vibrant student community and the concerns of long-term residents who want their neighbourhood to remain safe, clean, and well cared for. These are not abstract matters, they are the day-to-day realities of our streets, our homes, and our shared spaces.
While the times in which we live are challenging, I do not offer grand promises lightly made. Instead, I offer a straightforward pledge: to act with integrity, to weigh decisions carefully, and to keep your interests at the forefront of my work. That means taking seriously the issues of antisocial behaviour, ensuring our streets are properly maintained, supporting local businesses, and working for a fair balance between residents, students, and landlords alike.

Where there are difficulties, be it waste collection, community safety, or the pressures on local services I will meet them with openness. Where there is progress to be made, I will pursue it with determination, whether that is improving housing standards, strengthening community ties, or ensuring Fallowfield’s voice is clearly heard within the wider city.

Your confidence, should you grant it, will be met with diligence and accountability. And whether in agreement or disagreement, I will always value the right of every elector to be heard with fairness and respect. Please reach out to me should you have concerns to raise, ideas to share, or matters you wish to discuss; I will endeavour to listen carefully and respond thoughtfully, acting at all times in the best interests of Fallowfield and those who call it home.

I remain,
Yours faithfully,
Luke David Barker
Conservative and Unionist Party

I joined the Green Party in 2014 and have served on our local party committee since 2015, including two stints as Chair. I currently support our Green councillors as Press Officer.

I’m a charity communications professional and a member of the National Union of Journalists. I’ve worked across sectors including health, justice, housing, and arts and culture, giving me a wide range of experience that I would bring to Manchester Town Hall.

I’ve lived around Gorton and Abbey Hey since 2023 and can often be found running in Gorton Park. If elected, my priorities would be lowering the cost of living for residents, making local housing more affordable and energy-efficient, and supporting community spaces like Gorton Monastery and Hide Out Youth Zone.

Originally from the North East, Jackie came to Manchester to study for her PhD.

She was first elected to Manchester City Council for Gorton North in 1991 and served until 2012, during which she worked to ensure that Gorton District Centre was redeveloped, the Monastery was saved and to stop Tameside Council building around the reservoirs at Debdale.

Has lived in the Gorton Constituency for most of her adult life.  Jackie was involved in many campaigns to improve the Gorton area.

I first became active in politics as a 12 year old climate activist in Huddersfield fighting for action on the environmental emergencies that pose an existential threat to us all. Since then, I have been active in protest movements on issues including trans rights, justice for Palestinians and supporting striking workers. I am currently a second year politics student at Manchester Metropolitan University.

Since moving to Manchester, I have been focused on getting Greens elected locally and nationally. This has included in the recent Gorton and Denton by-election which saw the amazing Hannah Spencer elected to Parliament!

Local politics is about residents in Harpurhey. I will support them to tackle the cost of living crisis and fight for a Greener and cleaner area people can be proud of.
The local Conservative Unionist candidate for Harpurhey Manchester. One Nation Conservative

Thank you for taking the time to Look at my profile. My name is Martin power and I'm a member of United Kingdom Independence party I joined the party 2009 I was inspired by a conference that I attended at Torquay. I had decided to look at different political parties as I became very this appointed with the main parties I felt the didn't represent me.

The United Kingdom Independence party reminded me of what democracy is I was never comfortable been a member of the European Union. I was unhappy that on the elected politicians had a huge say in my day-to-day life our laws our courts. I decided that I too must get involved with UKIP to break away from the European Union and happily I was every part of that great event that did take place.

UKIP has evolved into a political force in British politics UKIP is not afraid to challenge the bigger parties on policies regarding   bettering our lives here in Britain. I feel it's a real privilege to be able to fight an election in 2017 as we are the only party that can make sure we do leave the European Union. We believe in an English parliament for English MPs to make decisions about England. We want to abolished a House of Lords as it is old antiquated and serves no real purpose in British democracy. We want to take control of our borders while we welcome emigration we want to be able to choose who comes into our country.We want to be rebuilt our army,  navy and air force, put more police on the streets, extra border patrol officers and extra prison officers. We want to abolish the Barnett formula where Scottish people get twice as much money per head from England than do English people. We want to make apprenticeships in skills just as important as University degrees. We would like to take back  10 billion foreign aid as we believe charity begins at home and we have an awful lot of problems in this country that needs to be addressed one been the overburdened NHS. We would continue to contribute to foreign aid and having

Hello,
My name is Dean Luke Booth and I am an Independent Candidate for Higher Blackley in the upcoming local election which will take place on the Thursday 7th May. A local election is simply an opportunity to choose the best candidate to represent your community and has little impact on central government. As an Independent I am not told how to vote by any political party so my focus will remain solely on improving Higher Blackley, supporting our community, acting on your behalf and fighting for the things that matter to you.
I have been a Higher Blackley resident my entire life so I am well aware of the issues we face. 
𝗠𝘆 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀
👮 To work with our local policing team to reduce crime and ensure 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲𝘆 is a safe & enjoyable place to live and work.
🍃 To ensure environmental issues are dealt with promptly, whilst protecting our parks & green spaces.
🫁 To promote health & wellbeing in 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲𝘆 through addiction dependency programs & community outreach projects.
🗣️ To communicate with residents on a regular basis to ensure they are aware of decisions, events and support available to them.
👂 To listen and act upon the needs of residents, community groups and businesses in 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲𝘆.
🚌 Fighting for much needed additional bus routes to help residents get to appointments, supermarkets like Aldi or the gym.
⚽️ Utilise community buildings in Higher Blackley to support the community and our children.
𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗜 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗽 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵:
❇️ Pot Holes
❇️ Fly Tipping
❇️ Blocked Drains
❇️ Environmental Issues
❇️ Pest Control
❇️ Road Markings
❇️ Road Signs
❇️ Street Lighting
❇️ Financial Advice
❇️ Housing Advice
❇️ Employment Advice
❇️ Bins & Waste Removal Services

You can follow my 𝗙𝗮𝗰𝗲𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗸 page for updates, events, advice etc - www.facebook.com/deanlukeboothHB. Email me deanlukebooth@gmail.com or call / text / WhatsApp me on 07999 437255.

I’d love the opportunity to work on your behalf. Is there anything I can do for you? Can I count on y

I grew up in Blackpool and have seen the effects of austerity first-hand. Like Blackpool, Higher Blackley has been abandoned by Westminster and taken for granted by successive governments. Since moving to Higher Blackley, I have felt an instant connection with the community and want to be a champion for its diverse constituency.

I’m proud to stand for the Green Party, representing those too often ignored or scapegoated by other parties. Too many young people are being left without clear pathways forward, we need to invest in opportunities and positive futures for young people in our community.

As a Green Party member, I’m a strong supporter of both green issues and social justice. I worked in Hulme for six years as an advice worker, when I must have helped at least a thousand residents with all kinds of problems, from homelessness, housing and debt to the criminal compensation scheme and benefits.

As a Hulme councillor, I’ll work just as hard on behalf of local people and the local community. My priorities will include tackling the rising cost of living by giving Manchester more affordable and energy-efficient homes and tackling climate change to create a healthier, cleaner city.

I’ll bring to my council work decades of experience on Manchester organisations, helping to manage: a local charity, a community association, a sports league, a members’ club and an environment network. I was a director of non-profit Hulme Community Computing and am a former Chair of Manchester City Council’s Environmental Forum.

As a Hulme councillor, I will work night and day to help local people and to champion local communities and wildlife. In Hulme, Robin has shown the Council let 1,312 mature trees be destroyed by developers over 20 years (2000-2020), that's two destroyed every week! Shockingly, there are no Tree Preservation Orders (TPOs) 
in Hulme Ward unless inside Conservation Areas.  As Hulme councillor, Robin will work hard to stop this nonsense.

Robin will reveal the shocking lack of proper crime data 
being published for Hulme Ward by Greater Manchester Police which they are required to do every month. 
1) GMP STOP-and-SEARCH – data MISSING 72 months!
2) GMP records of 14 categories of STREET CRIME – data MISSING 72 months!
According to police.uk, due to a change in Greater Manchester Police IT systems, police.uk received no street crime data, and no stop-and-search data from July 2019 onwards “The force is working to rectify this issue and provide the missing data over the coming months”. 
Compounding the problem, every month police.uk computers automatically relay the BLANK DATA to dozens of third parties, including people wanting to live in places with low crime rates, safe from muggings or car theft. Here is what crimerate.co.uk say on their “must see” website: "We are actively working to encourage 
more open data initiatives in the UK, since Greater Manchester Police do not maintain open data for their area".

As a councillor, Robin is keen to tackle air pollution.  His records show Manchester Metropolitan University has a long track record of being the regional hotspot for not only NO2 but also particulate air pollution. PM2.5 and PM10 cause muddled thinking leading to fewer passes in soccer matches, slower moves in chess and - alarmingly - lower grades in schools and universities.  Clean air is essential
 
Elsewhere, Robin created jobs around Wigan Pier; and protected dozens of ponds around Wigan, Standish and Coppull and he led Lancashire Newt Group negotiations to wi

Fesl grew up in Oldham. Like many young people from towns that had seen better days, he left to build a future elsewhere, earning a degree in International Relations through a bursary during his service with the British Army Reserve. That period shaped him — the physical resilience, the discipline, the understanding of what it actually means to serve others.

After the Army, Fesl started his civilian career in stock registry and banking, developing a practical understanding of financial markets before making a deliberate switch to public service. Working for Manchester City Council, he led projects across housing and regeneration that made a real difference to real people. He helped establish some of the first landlord licensing schemes in the country, designed to regulate and prosecute rogue landlords. He used Housing Market Renewal funding to bring substandard homes up to scratch for the most vulnerable residents in the city. He knows how local government works from the inside, and he knows where it too often falls short.

He is currently an entrepreneur running his own small business.

But what defines Fesl is not his career. It is how he shows up when his community needs him. In 2015, when severe floods devastated communities in Cumbria, Fesl travelled north with members of his community to help. They organised Christmas dinner and delivered presents to families who had lost everything. Nobody asked them to go. They just went.

On 22nd May 2017, the Manchester Arena attack changed this city. Fesl helped lead the Muslim community response through the Turn2Love initiative, making clear to anyone who was watching that terrorism finds no shelter in Manchester and that unity is stronger than division. He has carried that same instinct into every cause he has taken up since, from Ukraine to Kashmir to Palestine. It was his commitment to Palestinian human rights that brought him into the Green Party.

Since joining, Fesl co-founded Muslim Greens, and now serves on the

Living in Levenshulme / Listening In Levenshulme / Loving Levenshulme

This election is about Levenshulme. This is your chance to vote for positive change where we live. So why not vote for someone who lives in Levenshulme, loves Levenshulme and listens to you?
 I have lived here for over 20 years. Help me challenge the current council complacency so we can make Levenshulme better together. I’m tired of broken promises from Labour. Every year fly-tipping and litter and dumped cars get worse. We see this every day as residents. Labour councillors only see it when they visit the area.

We need housing people can afford to sustain our community. Labour supports the destruction of loved green space like our Secret Lakes and leaves sites that should be used for housing vacant and derelict. We were told funding for station access had been secured but it never happened. It is community action and pressure that will get us improvements, not Labour’s broken promises.

We live with the problems Labour has failed to solve. Levenshulme deserves better. I am here to deal with your problems locally.

What has Jeremy done already?
Listens to residents and deals with problems when people are ignored by councillors
Made Levenshulme Pride the biggest & best free local event of its kind in the region
Developed Levenshulme Community Directory promoting our groups & businesses
Published information during Covid in multiple languages to help keep us safe
Won the fight against council cuts for Arcadia Library & Leisure Centre and secured £600k to keep the Old Baths open while the new facilities were being built
Publicised the Active Neighbourhood road scheme and fought for improvements
Fought for Levenshulme Market when the council increased costs
Works with police to address crime, anti-social behaviour, and improve safety

I see the problems on our streets because I live here. Help me fight for our community with a strong, independent voice. If elected, I promise I will be out all year

I believe you don’t need public recognition to change lives, real impact happens quietly, by standing beside people in moments of uncertainty. That belief drives me to stand as the Green Party candidate for Longsight, to represent those who need support the most.

Longsight is home to many living with the everyday precarity of immigration status, language barriers, and systems that can feel distant and unresponsive. As an Immigration lawyer, I have become a trusted presence, helping residents often in their own language, navigate these challenges. From Uber drivers to shop workers, his work is rooted in care, dignity, and accessibility.

With strong ties to the ward and experience supporting people through complex situations, I’m standing to ensure local services are humane, accessible, and truly shaped around those they serve.

The march to another world war is a giant attack on the desire of working people to live in peace – and on our living standards, jobs and working conditions. Only the working class can prevent another global slaughter by taking political power into our own hands.

These are the central issues, not media hype on the turmoil within and between Labour, Conservative, Reform, Greens and other capitalist parties.

I’m honoured to be standing for the Greens in Miles Platting and Newton Heath.

I’m originally from Canada and have lived with my partner in the area since 2017. I currently work in hospitality at Co-op Live and the AO Arena.

I’m a dedicated social activist and have campaigned for many years on a range of important and urgent issues including climate action, social housing, universal education and healthcare, multiculturalism and corporate and governmental corruption.  I will bring my dedication to these issues to local politics'.

https://manchester.greenparty.org.uk/our-2026-local-election-candidates/#Miles-Platting

A bit more about me & why I'm running to be your next councillor, folks...

As someone who was born & raised in a different country, has previously worked in education - both in the UK & abroad - as well as having gained experience in the charity sector combatting poverty & preventing homelessness, I have a keen sense of the complex challenges many of us face in our daily lives.

I was previously involved with TfGM's sustainable journeys project, am a keen cyclist, love visiting my local public library, and regularly volunteer with my local community garden centre in neighbouring Failsworth, helping folks connect with nature, themselves & each other.

I have an intimate understanding of what it's really like living on a low income, working zero-hour contracts, struggling to pay the household bills & save money at the same time, and the personal & material sacrifices many of us have been forced to make to just make ends meet.

OUR FIVE KEY POLICY AREAS - MANCHESTER GREEN PARTY

All of our candidates will stand for national Green Party policies, which are decided democratically by members. Additionally, we have outlined five key policy areas that elected candidates will prioritise in the year ahead. These priorities are:

Tackle the climate crisis: 
- Ensure that Manchester City Council’s commitment to tackling climate change and biodiversity loss brin

My passion lays with social & climate justice, this has fuelled a lifetime of activism, from representing members as a trade union officer in the railway industry for over 20 years to becoming active in main stream politics, chairing local women committees and the legal justice system to most recently campaigning in local elections. "I truly understand the needs of my constituency. I have lived here since early childhood. I am raising my children here. I work here."

More about the candidate. Thirza Amina Asanga-Rae is a Nigeria born UK national. A Parent Governor at a local primary school where she also set up the PTA. As a Housing Community Organiser with GMTU, she has seen first hand the housing crisis we face.  Working in the railway industry she has decades of experience of the failing austerity in the transport sector. She co-founded a charity that seeks to stop the school to prison pipeline that young people face in inner cities. She focuses on the next generation as the key holders of tomorrow, and serves to be the representative for the next generation politicians.

Ms Asanga-Rae also holds other Executive and Directorships roles in UK: ■ Moss Side Hulme Community Development Trust ■ New Wave Trust ■ Central Association of Nigerians UK (CANUK) ■ Awera women’s league UK ( AWLUK)

I support the Green Party because I consider it is the only political party that offers hope and honest answers to the climate crisis and to the cost of living. More people nowadays feel pessimistic about the future, concerned about what kind of world we are leaving for our children and grandchildren. The invasion of Iran has brought into sharp focus how dependent we are on oil and gas being shipped around the globe just to provide the basics: food, heating and shelter. It’s not sustainable.

I’ve lived in New Moston for over thirty years and am a proud Mancunian, having been born and brought up, worked and raised a family here. I’ll be honoured to stand up for residents of my community on the council.

I’m a North West-born artist and community organiser with Nigerian roots, now rooted in Northenden for nearly nine of my 21 years in Manchester.

My work centres on bringing people together; championing those too often pushed to the margins, helping communities find their voice. I’m co-director of Rainbow Noir, have worked across film and TV, served on the Contact Theatre board, and delivered cultural competency training for the NHS, Home Office, Greater Manchester Combined Authority and Fire and Rescue Service. I can influence decision-makers, but I’m still someone you could have a pint with at The Farmer’s.

After serving as a member of Youth Parliament in my teens I swore off party politics for good, let down by a system built for and by the few.

The Green Party changed that. I’m standing because Northenden deserves practical, hopeful, community-rooted politics that turns our differences into strengths.

I want to help make Manchester a better place for us all. An inclusive, welcoming, thriving, clean, safe, and forward thinking city.

The council’s decisions matter in all sorts of ways. Issues such as bins, transport, planning, schools affect both individuals, families, the city, and the planet.

I’ve lived in Withington, adjoining Old Moat, for 15 years and am very much part of the local community. My children attend the local schools, we support the businesses in Withington Village, and always look out for our neighbours.

I’m a campaigner for road safety, active travel, and public transport. If elected I’d hope to be an asset to both the ward and the council at large in advocating for transport initiatives in the city.

I have stood as a Green Party candidate in the area, including most recently in the 2024 General Election, coming second. This time we are here to win.

Sebastian Moore serves as the SDP’s North West Chair. He lives in Old Moat and volunteers locally at a food bank. Having helped build the foundations of the SDP in the region, he is committed to forming a council which better represents the people of Manchester.

Sebastian Moore said:

“I’m standing in this election because the people of Old Moat deserve better. I love Manchester and I am proud to call this city my home but the council needs to start acting in the interests of its residents.

We are currently trying to copy London which will lead to a more precarious housing situation, more transience, and a lower trust community. We need to forge our own future as a city and that means electing councillors that will not grandstand on political issues outside of our control, but instead work to put the residents of Old Moat first, every time."

Having lived in many places, Manchester is the city I chose. Piccadilly introduced me to Manchester; from the quirky energy of the Northern Quarter to the heart of the Gay Village… This is home.

For many people, home feels increasingly fragile. Being working-class and having spent years working in mental health, I see the side of Piccadilly people miss – the struggle to get by.

I’m standing for the Greens because I want to turn my love for Manchester into positive action. I want to represent everyone who makes Piccadilly thrive. Let’s ensure this remains a place where everyone can afford a good life.

I live in the city centre with my husband and our dog, Annie. This is my home, and I’m standing because I want to make it a better place to live for the people who actually live and work here.

I’ve been campaigning on the issues residents raise every day: cleaner streets, safer public spaces, tackling anti-social behaviour like dangerous e-bikes and parcel theft, and reporting things like fly-tipping and dodgy pavements that too often get ignored.

I’ve also stood up for trans rights, raised money for humanitarian causes like the DEC’s Gaza appeal, and pushed for a more inclusive city where everyone feels they belong.

My 5 pledges:
1. Four new city centre green spaces and parks
2. Build genuinely affordable housing for local people
3. Cleaner streets and better everyday services
4. Stand by Trans and LGBTQ+ people, making our city a safe and inclusive place
5. Standing up for Labour’s true democratic socialist values locally

Manchester is growing fast,  but it needs to grow in a way that works for the people who live here right now.

I’m standing to be a councillor who shows up, listens, and gets things done.

I am a grassroots organiser and data-driven campaigner committed to fair rents, cleaner streets, and stronger, safer communities across Greater Manchester. With a background in forensic accounting and extensive experience in analytics and compliance, I use evidence to challenge inequality and push for practical, community-led solutions.

I champion community-rooted environmental action and work to broaden participation in local decisionmaking. I also volunteer to expand digital inclusion into deprived communities and have delivered health and inequality workshops in Manchester. This supports families facing systemic barriers while also helping residents gain essential skills to access services, education, and opportunities.

Whether analysing data or organising local action, I bring compassion, clarity, and a deep commitment to social justice.

I have a deep connection to Sharston, living here 50+ years and operated 2 local business's. I understand the unique challenges our residents and small businesses face. We need real action on
Fairer housing, 
Anti Social Behaviour 
Cost of living
Small buisness
Local economy
Im a local, fed up with the status quo, I will bring common sense solutions to the problems we face everyday,

Wythenshawe is a community of people that deserve so much more support than the Labour establishment is willing to give. In my day job, I design systems in software, but in my free time, I’m challenging the neglected systems that are failing us.

I joined the Green Party out of hope for a future that I’d be comfortable raising kids in, but I now don’t just have hope, I have belief. We can transition to systems that protect our natural environment, lift up those who have been left behind, and bring about a fairer and more just society again.

I joined the Green Party in early 2025 and quickly began creating content focused on raising awareness and solidarity around injustice across the world, from Gaza to Ukraine.

I soon gained experience as a campaign manager, before being inspired to put myself forward as a candidate following the Bournemouth party conference.

As a representative for Muslim Greens, I’m particularly keen to engage younger generations and members of the Asian community across Manchester. I was honoured to be awarded a Heraldic Badge by the Dame Robina Shah Foundation for service to young people’s education in February, presented by the Lord Mayor of Manchester.

As a candidate, I’m strongly committed to championing justice and integrity above all else, ensuring that my friends and neighbours across Whalley Range and beyond feel seen, heard and supported.

My name is Sameera Ashraf your candidate  for Bury South.  Thank you for joining us today. I am also thankful to our Party for entrusting me with this responsibility of being a voice for our community in the Parliament. We live in a Diverse community  which gives us opportunity to value each other and respect each other.  We all have suffered a lot in covid lost lots of friend and family .But the worst of any situation  or pandemic is what's happening in Palestine now a whole area on the map of the world is being wiped and everyone has a closed eye over it . The Worker's Party and George Galloway is  a ray of sunshine and only voice which demands a cease fire . I truly hope to be able to stand by my pledges and try to overcome the situations we face in our community and make a safer healthier and a greener environment for us all. Please support me support workers party and Vote for Truth vote for Gaza.

Manchester deserves better. The reason I am standing is to make this happen. The experience I have as a young person is one of communities that look out for one another in spite of politicians who do not. I have spent many hours volunteering for my local communities and have seen first hand how local organisations and councillors can make a difference to people’s lives.

If elected in Withington I will push for changes to how the authority can support an ecosystem of local organisations and empower individuals, showing them that they have an influence in how their local area evolves. Whilst I grew up in a rural town, the problems I saw there are not unique. I see people full of potential left behind due to a system that constantly fails them, that is why I chose to study and live in Withington, a community that I know can thrive.

April Preston grew up in a council house in Stockport with her two deaf and disabled parents. At the age of 10 she went into care under the local authority and now uses these life-experiences to forward progressive policy and thinking within the Liberal Democrats, focusing on the representation of those in challenging circumstances.

As a young girl she witnessed the devastating outcomes which comes from the mishandling of disability, mental health, and is determined to stop this happening to the next generation. She strives to support any person feeling ignored or alienated by the state.

She seeks to reach out to marginalised groups and young people who often go disregarded in politics such as care leavers, those with complex educational needs and LGBTQ+ people.

April is a Dachshund and indie pop enthusiast, and has been a part-time DJ on and off since she was 16, where her first event was a fundraiser for the Christie. 

As a keen feminist, she has chaired events and mentored other women in the Liberal Democrats, both in Manchester and across the country. She campaigns against the other party's tokenistic approach to the needs of women and strives to form policy which genuinely improves lives.

April is working to build a future in which the Liberal Democrats are the real alternative to a lazy Labour party and the cut-focussed Conservative party. She campaigned to remain in the EU and is concerned that a hard Brexit will be a threat to the least-well of people in Manchester.

Astrid Johnson is a German national who has lived in the UK for over 20 years. She has been actively involved in the Green Party as a committee member of her local Manchester Party since joining in 2014. In 2016 Astrid was one of the English delegates for the Green Party of England and Wales at the European Green Party Congress in Utrecht. She currently runs her own graphic design studio and works part-time as development manager for the Greater Manchester Law Centre, campaigning against legal aid cuts and austerity politics. She lives with her British husband and step-daughter in Moss Side, Manchester.

🗳️ **Vote Emily, 7th May - Your Independent for Woodhouse Park:**
I'm Emily Elżbieta Chomicz, a working class IT professional, a former mental health charity chair, book author, and independent investigative reporter for Queer Love Riot.

**🏙️ Vote Emily for ACCOUNTABILITY on Civic:**
- For nine months, I've investigated the regeneration, finding evidence of local traders being affected by gentrification. The council continually misleads the public with vague answers, and maintains a wall of silence on even basic questions.

- To this day, the council refuses to confirm if the planned 1,600 homes in the second phase of the regeneration, or the new Civic venues, will be legitimately affordable, using "commercial secrecy" on an 80% council-owned project to keep true costs from the public and protecting corporate interests instead, while our residents wait up to TWENTY years for a social home.

- I will demand at least 30% of new social homes to be for working age adults, and 30% of new homes built to be social homes. I will get to the bottom of this mess and prioritise people's voices in this key project for Woodhouse Park, rather than accept top-down planning by the government.

**🛏️ Vote Emily for SCRUTINY in Housing:**
- No one should be left out in the cold due to repeated failures to manage local housing waiting lists. As someone who has been homeless in Manchester three times, I know firsthand how it feels to be repeatedly failed over and over. I will directly challenge Manchester City Council on unfair housing register decisions and policies.

- Nobody should suffer in unfit, cold, mould-ridden housing. It’s hardly in the spirit of the Wythenshawe we love. I will work with the Wythenshawe Community Housing Group to scrutinise maintenance work, urgently identify properties needing repair, and direct any local financial assistance to be also given to homeowners in poverty.

**🏥 Vote Emily to SAFEGUARD the future:**
- As kids have nowhere to go in the real world,
